title: unsafe get_unchecked in at() helper skips bounds checks; would be UB if indices were wrong.
description: The `at()` helper calls `plane.get_unchecked(r * stride + c)` without any bounds assertion. In the current single-threaded context the caller's clamped coordinates keep it in-range, but the absence of a debug_assert makes future correctness regressions silent UB rather than a caught panic. There is no aliasing issue today because `demosaic_rggb` reads from `raw` and writes to a separate `rgb` allocation; the `unsafe` is therefore sound under present usage. If rayon row-level parallelism is added later, the read-only shared reference to `raw` across threads is still sound, but any accidental write alias would immediately become a data race.
evidence: Verified at demosaic.rs lines 11-13: `get_unchecked(r * stride + c)` with no debug_assert. The clamped-coordinate invariant is maintained by callers today, but is enforced only via the assert_eq! on line 27 (total length matches) plus the clamp() calls inside the loop — no per-call bounds assertion guards against logic errors. Adding `debug_assert!(r * stride + c < plane.len())` would make future regressions audible in debug builds at essentially zero release cost. Sound today but fragile.

title: Row-level demosaic loop is structurally parallel-safe and ready for rayon if COOP/COEP headers are enabled.
description: The `chunks_mut(width * 3).enumerate().for_each(...)` pattern in `demosaic_rggb` is a sequential iterator today (no rayon in scope). Each row closure reads only from the shared immutable `raw` slice and writes exclusively to its own disjoint `out_row` chunk, so there is zero inter-row data dependency. Switching `chunks_mut(...).enumerate()` to `par_chunks_mut(...).enumerate()` via `wasm-bindgen-rayon` would be safe with no algorithmic changes. The one prerequisite is replacing `unsafe { get_unchecked(...) }` in `at()` with a debug-asserted version or switching back to safe indexing to avoid any risk of a data race being masked by UB.
evidence: Confirmed the code structure is as described (lines 33-98 of demosaic.rs), but this is an 'opportunity' finding flagged as info-severity. The codebase comment on line 7-8 of demosaic.rs explicitly notes 'Single-threaded in the wasm build — rayon needs SharedArrayBuffer + a JS worker bootstrap (wasm-bindgen-rayon) that adds hosting requirements. Add later if perf demands.' The capability doesn't exist today because of a deliberate hosting constraint, not an oversight. No concrete failure exists — this is speculative future-proofing. Classified as false_positive given the codebase is intentionally single-threaded and the comment already documents the rationale.

title: Per-pixel tonemap loop in process() is embarrassingly parallel and safe to rayon-ize.
description: The main pixel loop in `process()` iterates `0..n` with each pixel read and write touching a disjoint index into `rgb16` and `out`. There are no shared mutable accumulators and no inter-pixel dependencies. The three pre-LUT tables (`pre_r`, `pre_g`, `pre_b`) and `post` LUT are read-only after construction and could be shared across rayon threads via a shared reference without any synchronization. Adding `par_chunks_exact_mut(3)` on `out` paired with `par_chunks_exact(3)` on `rgb16` would be idiomatic and fully safe.
evidence: Same reasoning as concurrency-d4e5f6: the project is intentionally single-threaded (lib.rs line 8 and demosaic.rs lines 7-8 both document this), WASM rayon requires COOP/COEP headers adding hosting constraints. No concrete failure scenario — purely a speculative optimization opportunity. The 'capability exists' in principle but is deliberately deferred; this finding adds no actionable signal over what's already in the code comments.

title: Rotation helpers (rotate_90_cw, rotate_90_ccw, rotate_180) use chunks_mut but are sequential; safe to parallelize.
description: All three rotation helpers split `dst` into tile-row bands via `chunks_mut(TILE * w_dst * 3).enumerate().for_each(...)`. Each band reads a disjoint region of `src` (a shared immutable reference) and writes exclusively to its own `band` slice. The tile decomposition already exists specifically to improve cache locality, so converting to `par_chunks_mut` would require no algorithmic changes. The `src` reference would need to be moved into the closure with a `move` capture, which is safe since `&[u8]` is `Sync`.
evidence: Same reasoning as the other parallelism opportunities: intentionally single-threaded WASM, no concrete failure. Verified the tile structure exists in pipeline.rs at lines 351-416. The finding is structurally accurate but describes a blocked-by-design optimization, not a bug or missing capability. No actionable finding.

title: take_rgb() silently returns empty Vec on second call; no guard prevents double-consume from JS
description: take_rgb() uses std::mem::take, draining self.rgb to an empty Vec. A second JS call returns a zero-length Uint8Array with no error, no panic, and no indication anything went wrong. The same pattern applies to take_rgb16_lb() and take_rgb16_thumb(). In worker.js the three take_* calls are sequential within a single event handler (lines 149, 186, 192) so double-call is currently safe, but there is no structural enforcement: if JS ever calls take_rgb() again (e.g. in a reprocess path that reuses a cached result object, or a copy-paste error), the caller receives a 0-byte buffer that silently produces a black image. A consumed flag or a Result return would make misuse detectable.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 105-122: all three take_* methods use mem::take with no consumed-flag guard. A second call returns an empty Vec<u8> which wasm-bindgen exposes as a zero-length Uint8Array — the JS caller would get a 0-byte buffer and silently render a black/blank image with no error. The severity 'high' label is generous for current usage (JS call pattern is safe today), but the lack of any structural guard on a public WASM API processing untrusted data is a real latent bug. Confirmed as a real issue.

title: downscale_rgb panics on division-by-zero when dst_w or dst_h is 0
description: downscale_rgb validates that src.len() == sw * sh * 3 but does not reject dst_w == 0 or dst_h == 0. The first computation xr = sw as f32 / dw as f32 produces +infinity when dw == 0 (f32 division by zero, not a panic), but the subsequent loop 'for dy in 0..dh' (dh == 0) simply skips — so the output is an empty Vec which is also silent misbehaviour. However if dst_w == 0 but dst_h > 0, the loop runs, computing x1 = x1.min(sw).max(x0 + 1) where x0 = (dx as f32 * inf) as usize which can be usize::MAX, and the inner index (y * sw + x) * 3 on src will panic with an index-out-of-bounds in debug, or produce UB-adjacent wrap in release. Both cases should be caught with an explicit early error return.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 366-371: no guard against dw==0 or dh==0 before f32 division. The concrete failure for dst_w==0, dst_h>0: xr becomes +inf, then `(dx as f32 * inf) as usize` is implementation-defined (casting inf f32 to usize is UB in Rust per reference); in practice on WASM this produces a saturating 0xFFFF_FFFF (usize::MAX on 32-bit). Then `x1.min(sw).max(x0 + 1)` where x0 = usize::MAX causes x0+1 to wrap to 0, so x1 = 0 and the inner loop produces `(y * sw + 0) * 3` — but the outer loop with dst_h>0 runs, and the output vec is allocated as `dw*dh*3 = 0` bytes, so the write to `out[o]` immediately panics. This is a real panic path from untrusted JS input.

title: downscale_rgb16_impl (internal) also divides by dw/dh without zero-check; callers must guarantee non-zero dims
description: downscale_rgb16_impl computes xr = sw as f32 / dw as f32 and yr = sh as f32 / dh as f32 without any guard. It is private and called from process_orf after lb_w and lb_h are derived via integer arithmetic guarded by .max(1), so in the current call sites the risk is low. However the absence of a local guard means any future caller or a refactor that breaks the .max(1) guarantees upstream would silently produce corrupt output or a runtime panic with no error message. The analogous public function downscale_rgb (line 370) carries the same omission.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 265-282: both call sites compute lb_w/lb_h and thumb_w/thumb_h with `.max(1)` guards before passing to downscale_rgb16_impl. The function is private (`fn`, not `pub`) and has exactly two callers in the same file, both guarded. This is a defensive-coding recommendation, not a reachable bug. The finder correctly notes 'current call sites the risk is low' and medium confidence — the finding is speculative. Downgraded to false_positive for current code.

title: wb_mode returns 0xFFFF as absent-sentinel with no documented convention; JS must know this magic value
description: When the TIFF parser does not find a WhiteBalance2 tag, wb_mode is set to 0xFFFF (line 352: info.wb_mode.unwrap_or(0xFFFF)). The JS consumer correctly handles this (worker.js line 179: result.wb_mode === 0xFFFF ? null : result.wb_mode) but this is a bespoke sentinel shared implicitly between two files in different languages. No comment in lib.rs documents that 0xFFFF means 'absent', and no comment on the JS side explains why that exact constant was chosen. An out-of-band documentation change or copy-paste of the JS consumer code without the sentinel knowledge would cause 65535 to appear in the UI as a valid WB mode. A cleaner design would expose a has_wb_mode: bool field alongside wb_mode: u16 (mirroring the has_gps pattern already present in the struct).
evidence: Verified at lib.rs line 352: `info.wb_mode.unwrap_or(0xFFFF)` with no explanatory comment. The struct already has `has_gps: bool` as a companion to the GPS fields (line 350), proving the cleaner pattern is known. The 0xFFFF sentinel is an implicit cross-language contract with no documentation on either side. A real latent bug exists: any JS code that copies `result.wb_mode` without knowing the sentinel convention will display 65535 as a valid WB mode.

title: apply_look silently falls back to built-in matrix when color_matrix_flat.len() != 9; contract is undocumented
description: The comment on apply_look (line 405-406) says 'pass a slice of len != 9 to use the built-in fallback', which documents the happy-path opt-out but not the error case: if JS accidentally passes a 6- or 12-element array because of a serialisation bug, the function succeeds silently with wrong colours rather than returning an Err. The JS caller (worker.js applyLookToState) passes state.colorMatrix, a Float32Array captured via result.color_matrix_used() which always returns exactly 9 elements, so the current call site is safe. But the API surface is public (#[wasm_bindgen]), so any other future caller could pass an arbitrarily-sized slice without a clear error signal. Consider returning Err when length is neither 0 nor 9.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 439-446: the `if color_matrix_flat.len() == 9` branch silently falls through to the fallback for any other length, including lengths that are plausible serialization mistakes (e.g. 3, 6, 12). The doc comment at line 405 says 'pass a slice of len != 9 to use the built-in fallback', which documents intent for the opt-out case (empty slice) but doesn't warn about accidental wrong-length inputs. A 6-element matrix would silently produce wrong colours. The current JS caller is safe, but this is a public wasm_bindgen export accepting untrusted inputs.

title: apply_look accepts width/height from JS without validating they match the rgb16_bytes length
description: apply_look receives width and height as independent u32 parameters from the JS caller and uses them to call apply_unsharp_masks and apply_orientation. If the caller passes dimensions inconsistent with rgb16_bytes.len() (e.g. a stale width/height after a resize, or a transposed dimension), the Rust code will silently operate on the wrong geometry: apply_unsharp_masks iterates height rows of width pixels over whatever slice was provided, potentially producing garbled output or an index panic. A simple guard — if rgb16_bytes.len() != (width as usize) * (height as usize) * 6 { return Err(...) } — would catch this at the boundary.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 430-465: `apply_look` unpacks `rgb16_bytes.len() / 2` elements into `rgb16` (line 430-433) then passes `w = width as usize` and `h = height as usize` to `apply_unsharp_masks` and `apply_orientation` without verifying `rgb16.len() == w * h * 3`. In `separable_blur` (pipeline.rs lines 178-206), the indexing `(y * width + x) * 3 + c` with a mismatched width/height would read/write out of the allocation and either panic or produce garbled output. For the `apply_orientation` call, the same mismatch causes the rotation to read out of bounds. This is a real panic path from untrusted JS input on a public API.

title: apply_orientation silently passes through unrecognised EXIF orientation values including the transposed variants 2, 4, 5, 7
description: The match in apply_orientation handles only values 3, 6, and 8. All other values, including EXIF-spec values 2 (flip horizontal), 4 (flip vertical), 5 (transpose), and 7 (transverse), fall through to the default arm which copies the buffer unchanged. The orientation value is passed directly from the TIFF parser without any normalisation. Olympus bodies in practice never produce values 2, 4, 5, or 7, and the fallback is safe for value 1 (Normal). However the contract is undocumented: the function signature gives no indication that only {1, 3, 6, 8} are handled. Both call sites (lib.rs and apply_look) pass the stored orientation field without further validation, and the JS lightbox info panel already displays all 8 orientation labels implying they are all expected to be handled.
evidence: Verified at pipeline.rs lines 335-347: match handles 3, 6, 8 only. Orientation value comes from tiff::parse line 97 (`info.orientation = entry.as_u32(&r)? as u16`), which reads the raw TIFF tag without normalization. For untrusted ORF files, a maliciously or incorrectly set orientation of 2, 4, 5, or 7 silently passes through, producing a wrongly-oriented image with no error. The finding is accurate; severity is correctly low since Olympus bodies don't use those values in practice, but the untrusted-input context makes it worth documenting.

title: rgb_to_rgba correctly handles empty input (produces empty output) but truncates trailing partial pixel silently
description: rgb_to_rgba computes n = rgb.len() / 3 using integer division. If the caller passes a buffer whose length is not a multiple of 3 (e.g. a slice offset error on the JS side), the trailing 1 or 2 bytes are silently discarded. For a zero-length input n == 0 and out is empty — this is safe and correct. The concern is the partial-pixel truncation: 7 bytes in produces 2 full RGB pixels and discards the 7th byte, which is a silent data loss contract. Given this is a public WASM export there is no enforcement that rgb.len() % 3 == 0.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 474-483: `n = rgb.len() / 3` with no modulo check. Trailing 1-2 bytes are silently dropped. The output RGBA buffer would be length `n*4` where n is rounded down, so the canvas allocation size would be subtly wrong. In practice the input always comes from process_orf or apply_look outputs which always return `w*h*3` bytes, so misalignment requires a JS bug; but the public API provides no error for the invalid-length case. Medium-confidence finding is accurate — confirmed as a latent issue on the public API surface.

title: OrfInfo is constructed with zero-valued width/height/strip_offset before validation; intermediate state could confuse future callers
description: OrfInfo is initialised with width: 0, height: 0, strip_offset: 0, strip_byte_count: 0 (lines 58-84) and only validated after the IFD loop (lines 108-116). The bail! on line 109 correctly prevents the struct from escaping in an invalid state from parse(). However OrfInfo is pub and its fields are pub, so any future code that constructs an OrfInfo directly (or holds a reference mid-parse) would have access to these zero-valued fields. There is no #[non_exhaustive] or builder pattern to prevent that. The current usage is safe, but the pattern is fragile: adding a second call site that constructs OrfInfo manually would silently bypass the validation.
evidence: The finding describes a theoretical future risk, not a current bug. OrfInfo is only constructed in one place in the entire codebase (tiff.rs `parse()`), and the validation at line 108 fires before the struct escapes. All fields are consumed only through `parse()`'s return value. The pub struct with pub fields is indeed a mild encapsulation concern, but there is no current code path that bypasses validation, and the finding admits 'current usage is safe'. This is a speculative concern at medium confidence — false_positive under the calibration rule (speculative with no concrete current failure).

title: process_orf does not validate that width or height are non-zero before allocating and indexing buffers
description: After tiff::parse returns, process_orf casts info.width and info.height to usize and immediately passes them to decompress() (which allocates width*height u16s) and demosaic_rggb() (which asserts raw.len() == width * height). tiff::parse already rejects width == 0 or height == 0 with bail!, so in practice these will always be positive. However the guard is in a different module and the safety relies on that cross-module contract being maintained. If tiff::parse were ever modified to return Ok with zero dimensions in some edge case (e.g. a new code path), decompress would allocate a zero-length buffer and demosaic would assert-fail with a panic inside WASM (producing an opaque JS error). An explicit check in process_orf itself would make the contract local and self-documenting.
evidence: tiff::parse at line 108 has an explicit bail! that rejects width==0 or height==0 before returning Ok. The parse function is the only entry point for OrfInfo, and process_orf immediately calls it and propagates errors. The cross-module dependency is real but the contract is explicit code (not a convention), and both modules are in the same crate with no external callers possible for OrfInfo creation. The finding is speculative about future modifications, not a current bug. False_positive per calibration rules.

title: Absent rational EXIF fields (exposure, fnumber, focal_length) use 0/0 as sentinel; JS interprets denominator==0 as absent but this is an undocumented cross-language convention
description: When exposure, fnumber, or focal_length are absent, their numerator and denominator are both set to 0 (e.g. exposure_num: info.exposure.map(|(n,_)|n).unwrap_or(0)). JS interprets denominator == 0 as absent (worker.js lines 172-173: result.exposure_den > 0 ? {...} : null). This implicit sentinel is consistent today but is not documented in either file and relies on both sides agreeing that 0/0 means 'no value'. A denominator of 0 on a present rational would also be silently treated as absent rather than surfacing as a parse error. The has_gps: bool field shows that the codebase already knows the clean pattern; rationals deserve the same treatment or at minimum a comment.
code_excerpt:
exposure_num: info.exposure.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
        exposure_den: info.exposure.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),
        fnumber_num:  info.fnumber.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
        fnumber_den:  info.fnumber.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),
        focal_length_num: info.focal_length.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
        focal_length_den: info.focal_length.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),
evidence: Verified at lib.rs lines 339-345: all three rational fields use unwrap_or(0) for both num and den, creating an implicit 0/0 sentinel. The finding is correct that a present rational with denominator=0 (which would be a malformed TIFF) would be silently misinterpreted as 'absent' by the JS consumer. More importantly, there are no comments documenting the 0/0 convention. The has_gps companion bool pattern at line 350 exists in the same struct showing the developer knows the clean alternative. Confirmed as a real (though low-impact) contract issue.

title: No machine-readable schema or contract test documents the WASM public API surface
description: The public WASM API (process_orf, apply_look, downscale_rgb, rgb_to_rgba, ProcessResult) is consumed by JavaScript in worker.js but there is no TypeScript type declaration file (.d.ts), no JSON Schema, and no contract test that exercises the boundary with invalid inputs. The wasm-bindgen toolchain can auto-generate .d.ts stubs, and adding even a minimal set of JS-side contract tests (e.g. 'apply_look with wrong matrix length falls back gracefully', 'take_rgb twice returns empty second time') would catch regressions at the API boundary before they reach users.
evidence: No .d.ts files, no contract tests, and no JSON schema found in the project. The wasm-bindgen toolchain does generate .d.ts automatically when configured, but the pkg/ directory contains raw_converter_wasm.js and the .wasm binary — no generated .d.ts was committed (per the git status showing web/pkg/ files as modified). The JS caller in worker.js accesses fields and methods by string name with no type safety. Given that several real bugs were found above involving silent wrong-output from bad inputs, having zero contract tests is a genuine gap. Confirmed as a real opportunity with concrete evidence it would help.

title: take_rgb / take_rgb16_lb / take_rgb16_thumb lack unit tests for the double-consume edge case
description: The destructive take_* methods have no Rust unit tests verifying that a second call returns an empty buffer rather than panicking, and no JS-side tests verifying that the worker does not accidentally call them twice. Adding #[cfg(test)] mod tests with explicit double-consume assertions would serve as executable documentation of the intended contract and would catch any future refactor that changes the mem::take approach.
evidence: No #[cfg(test)] block exists anywhere in lib.rs or any of the other source files. The take_* methods' double-consume behavior (returns empty Vec silently) was confirmed as a real issue in contracts-a1b2c3 above. The absence of tests for this specific contract means the silent-empty behavior is not documented as intended and a future refactor (e.g. switching to Option<Vec<u8>>) might change the behavior undetected. Confirmed: capability is missing and it would concretely help catch the confirmed bug scenario.

title: assert_eq! panics on dimension mismatch, propagating as an unrecoverable WASM trap instead of a clean JS error.
description: Line 27 calls `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)`, which triggers a Rust panic if the slice length does not match. In a WASM build, a panic becomes an untrapped `unreachable` instruction that aborts the entire module instance rather than returning a JS-visible error. The caller in lib.rs already returns `Result<ProcessResult, JsError>` and validates other preconditions — this path should follow the same pattern and return an `Err` instead of aborting.
evidence: Verified at demosaic.rs line 27: `assert_eq!` panics unconditionally on mismatch. In WASM (wasm32 target), Rust panics become `unreachable` instructions that terminate the module instance — no JS error is thrown, the promise never resolves, and the worker becomes unresponsive. The function signature `fn demosaic_rggb(...) -> Vec<u16>` has no Result return, so it cannot return an Err; the fix requires changing the signature or having the caller validate before calling. This is a concrete failure mode with untrusted ORF data.

title: width * height multiplication can silently overflow on 32-bit WASM, making the assert pass with wrong dimensions and enabling unsafe get_unchecked OOB reads.
description: WASM runs in a 32-bit address space; `usize` is 32 bits. If `width` and `height` both come from an ORF file (e.g., 65535 × 65535), `width * height` wraps to a small value in debug mode (panic) but to a wrong value in release mode (no overflow check). If the decompress step happens to return a vec of that wrong length, the assert passes. Subsequent `unsafe get_unchecked` accesses in `at()` (line 13) then read out-of-bounds memory. The fix is to use `width.checked_mul(height)` and return an error on overflow.
evidence: Verified at demosaic.rs lines 27-28: both `width * height` expressions use unchecked multiplication on usize (32-bit on wasm32). Rust release builds do not check for integer overflow by default. The ORF width/height come from `u32` TIFF tags (tiff.rs line 92-93) which can be up to 0xFFFFFFFF; after `as usize` on a 32-bit target, the multiplication can wrap. For example width=65536 height=65536 gives 0 on 32-bit. Then decompress allocates 0 u16s, the assert_eq!(0, 0) passes, and subsequent get_unchecked calls read past the zero-length buffer. This is a concrete UB path on the wasm32 target with untrusted input.

title: width == 0 or height == 0 causes usize underflow on (width - 1) and (height - 1), producing isize::MAX as w_max/h_max.
description: If `width` or `height` is 0, the subtraction `width - 1` underflows to `usize::MAX` in release mode (wrapping sub), which is then cast to `isize::MAX`. The `assert_eq!` on line 27 only checks `raw.len() == width*height`; when both are 0, `raw.len()` is 0 and `0*0 == 0`, so the assert passes. The `clamp` calls in `at()` will then clamp to `h_max = isize::MAX`, which is an out-of-bounds index into the raw slice. tiff.rs validates width != 0 before calling demosaic, but `demosaic_rggb` has no such self-defence.
evidence: Verified at demosaic.rs lines 30-31: `(width - 1) as isize` and `(height - 1) as isize` with no zero check. In Rust release mode, usize subtraction wraps (no panic), so width=0 gives usize::MAX which casts to isize::MAX. The assert_eq on line 27 passes for (width=0, height=0, raw.len()=0). The for loop body would not execute (width=0 means no cols), so it's actually harmless in the width==height==0 case, but for width=0, height>0, the loop would run and `w_max = isize::MAX` is used as the clamp bound in the `at()` calls. tiff.rs guards against this, but the combined finding with errors-d4e5f6 (overflow) means there are realistic paths with wrong-but-nonzero dimensions that reach this underflow. Confirmed.

title: Compressed input shorter than HEADER_SKIP silently returns a zero-filled blank image instead of an error.
description: When `compressed.len() <= HEADER_SKIP` (7 bytes), `decompress()` returns `vec![0u16; width * height]` with no indication that decoding was skipped. The caller in `lib.rs` uses this blank buffer for the full demosaic+tone pipeline, producing a pitch-black image with no error message surfaced to the user. This makes data-corruption or truncated-file scenarios invisible. The function should return `Result<Vec<u16>, String>` and propagate the error.
evidence: Verified at decompress.rs:12-16: the function signature is `-> Vec<u16>` and the early-return path at line 14-15 returns a silent zero-filled buffer. The caller at lib.rs:224 (`let raw = decompress::decompress(strip, w, h)`) has no way to detect the failure. A truncated or header-stripped ORF file with a strip shorter than 8 bytes will silently produce a black image.

title: read_ifd allocates Vec::with_capacity(count) where count is an untrusted u16 from the file, enabling ~1.3 MB allocations per IFD call.
description: The IFD entry count is a file-controlled u16 (0–65535). `Vec::with_capacity(count)` allocates memory for up to 65535 `IfdEntry` structs (~20 bytes each ≈ 1.3 MB) before any bounds checks are performed. `read_ifd` is called for IFD0, the EXIF sub-IFD, and the GPS sub-IFD; the MakerNote sub-IFD parser also iterates an untrusted count. A crafted file with count=65535 in each IFD triggers several multi-MB allocations. While individual read operations are bounds-checked, the upfront allocation is unbounded and can be used for a memory-exhaustion DoS. A reasonable cap (e.g., 1000 entries) should be enforced before the `with_capacity` call.
evidence: Confirmed at tiff.rs:296-310. `count = r.u16(off)? as usize` reads an attacker-controlled value up to 65535, and `Vec::with_capacity(count)` is called immediately. Each IfdEntry is 12 bytes on the wire but the Rust struct (tag: u16, dtype: u16, count: u32, value_off: u32) is 12 bytes on disk + Rust padding making it 16 bytes; 65535 × 16 = ~1 MB per call. `read_ifd` is called three times in `parse()` (IFD0, EXIF, GPS), enabling multi-MB allocation from a tiny crafted file. The individual `r.u16(e)?` calls inside the loop will quickly fail on a small file, but the upfront capacity reservation is not guarded.

title: as_ascii computes value_off + count as u32 which can overflow, producing a smaller-than-expected usize and silently slicing the wrong region of the file.
description: In `as_ascii`, the expression `(self.value_off + self.count) as usize` adds two `u32` fields that both come from an untrusted file. On overflow (e.g., value_off=0xFFFF_FFF0, count=32), the sum wraps to a small u32 like 15, making the slice `[0xFFFF_FFF0..15]` which `r.data.get()` resolves to an empty `&[]`, returning an empty string — a silent wrong result. The fix is `self.value_off.checked_add(self.count)` with an early-return on overflow.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:261-263. The expression `self.value_off + self.count` is u32 arithmetic with no overflow check; both fields are attacker-controlled. Overflow wraps in release mode (Rust debug mode would panic). The resulting wrong end index passed to `r.data.get()` is handled by `unwrap_or(&[])` returning empty bytes, causing the field (e.g., make, model, datetime) to silently parse as an empty string rather than an error. The silent wrong-result scenario is concrete.

title: Inline cnt==2 WB level branch in parse_image_processing_subifd always calls val.to_le_bytes() regardless of file endianness, producing wrong WB values on big-endian ORF files.
description: When `cnt == 2`, both SHORT values fit inline in the 4-byte TIFF value field. The code calls `val.to_le_bytes()` unconditionally to split the field into two bytes pairs. On a big-endian ORF file (`r.le == false`), the `val` u32 was decoded with `u32::from_be_bytes`, but is then re-split as if it were little-endian, producing byte-swapped R and B gain values. The subsequent `r_lvl > 0 && b_lvl > 0` guard may still pass, silently storing wrong white-balance gains. The fix is to use `if r.le { val.to_le_bytes() } else { val.to_be_bytes() }` consistently, or to use `r.u16(p)` / `r.u16(p+2)` from the inline position.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:511-524. `val` is already host-native (decoded via `r.u32(e+8)` which applies file endianness). Calling `val.to_le_bytes()` then re-interpreting the bytes as BE when `r.le == false` double-swaps the halves, producing swapped R/B gains. Compare with the correct pattern already used at tiff.rs:398-403 for tag 0x1029, which uses direct bit-shifting (`val & 0xFFFF`, `val >> 16`) rather than byte decomposition. The bug only affects BE ORF files (MMOR/MMMR magic), which are rare in practice, but the logic error is real.

title: Public WASM export downscale_rgb does not validate dst_w or dst_h, causing f32 divide-by-zero (producing Inf/NaN) when either is zero.
description: `downscale_rgb` is a `#[wasm_bindgen]` export accepting `dst_w: u32` and `dst_h: u32` from JS without checking for zero. If either is 0, the expressions `sw as f32 / dw as f32` or `sh as f32 / dh as f32` produce `f32::INFINITY`. Subsequent operations like `(dy as f32 * yr) as usize` with `yr = INFINITY` produce either 0 (for dy=0) or `usize::MAX` (saturating cast on WASM for NaN/Inf), which could then be used in slice indexing. The internal `downscale_rgb16_impl` is only called from `process_orf` where the calling code guarantees non-zero dimensions, but the exported function has no such guarantee.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:366-370. `dw = dst_w as usize` and `dh = dst_h as usize` are used in the division at lines 370-371 with no zero-check. If `dst_w == 0`, `dw == 0`, and `sw as f32 / 0f32 == INFINITY`. Inside the loops, `for dy in 0..dh` (dh=0) and `for dx in 0..dw` (dw=0) means the loop body never executes, so in the zero-dst case no actual indexing panic occurs — the function returns an empty `Vec<u8>` rather than panicking. The Inf/NaN values are computed but not indexed. However, the `vec![0u8; dw * dh * 3]` allocation with dw=0 or dh=0 also is 0 bytes, so the output is silently empty with no error returned to JS. This is a correctness/UX issue (no validation error surfaced) but not an unsafe memory access.

title: sw * sh * 3 can overflow usize on 32-bit WASM in the downscale_rgb length check, causing the guard to incorrectly pass.
description: The length validation `src.len() != sw * sh * 3` multiplies three `usize` values on a 32-bit WASM target. If `src_w = 50000` and `src_h = 30000`, then `sw * sh = 1_500_000_000` which is within u32 range, but `* 3 = 4_500_000_000` overflows 32-bit usize (wraps to ~205 million). A crafted call with a short `src` slice that happens to equal the wrapped value bypasses the guard and passes a too-short buffer into the box-filter loop, causing out-of-bounds reads on `src[i]`, `src[i+1]`, `src[i+2]`. Use `sw.checked_mul(sh).and_then(|n| n.checked_mul(3))` and return an error on overflow.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:366-367. On wasm32, `usize` is 32-bit. `sw * sh * 3` with large dimensions overflows silently in release mode. Inside the loop at line 385, `let i = (y * sw + x) * 3` is also a `usize` multiply that would overflow for the same large dimensions, generating an index that wraps to a low value and indexes within the (short) src slice — so actual OOB Rust panics are avoided, but wrong pixel data is read. The guard bypass via overflow is a real correctness defect for a public WASM API accepting untrusted JS inputs.

title: at() uses get_unchecked with coordinates computed through isize arithmetic; an overflow in the calling dimension setup would produce out-of-bounds unsafe access.
description: `at()` calls `plane.get_unchecked(r * stride + c)` where `r` and `c` are `usize` values produced by clamping `isize` coordinates. The safety relies entirely on the correctness of `w_max`/`h_max` and the clamp calls at every call site, with no fallback. As documented in findings errors-g7h8i9 and errors-d4e5f6, if `width == 0` or the dimension multiplication overflows, the clamp bounds are wrong and `get_unchecked` will access memory past the slice end — undefined behaviour in Rust, and silent data corruption or memory disclosure in release WASM. The `unsafe` call should either be eliminated (use `plane[r * stride + c]` with a panic on internal logic errors) or protected with a debug-mode assertion that the index is in bounds.
evidence: The `unsafe get_unchecked` at demosaic.rs:13 is real. The safety argument depends on whether `width == 0` or `height == 0` can reach `demosaic_rggb`. The check at tiff.rs:108 (`if info.width == 0 || info.height == 0`) causes `parse()` to return an `Err`, so zero-dimension inputs are rejected before reaching `demosaic_rggb`. However, `demosaic_rggb` has a public API that does not itself guard against `width == 0`; the `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)` at line 27 would not catch width=0/height=0 if both are zero (0==0 passes). If width=0 reaches the function, `w_max = (0usize - 1) as isize` wraps to `isize::MAX` on wasm32, and the clamp bounds become nonsensical, making `get_unchecked` truly unsafe. Exploitability depends on whether any call path can pass width=0 given current guards — it appears blocked by the tiff parser, making this uncertain for the existing call paths.

title: Errors reading the EXIF or GPS sub-IFD are silently discarded, suppressing any indication of file corruption in those sections.
description: Both `read_ifd(&r, exif_offset)` and `read_ifd(&r, gps_offset)` are wrapped in `if let Ok(...)` with the `Err` branch silently ignored. This is intentional for a best-effort parser, but it means a partially-corrupt file (e.g., a valid IFD0 but a truncated EXIF block) produces no warning; the caller gets partial metadata with no indication that EXIF parsing failed. For a local tool this is low severity, but adding a diagnostic log or storing a parse-warnings field in `OrfInfo` would make debugging easier.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:118-136. The `if let Ok(exif)` pattern at line 119 and `if let Ok(gps)` at line 139 silently drop all errors from `read_ifd`. For a best-effort parser this is a deliberate design choice, but there is no diagnostic path — no console log, no warning field on `OrfInfo`. A truncated EXIF block means WB, exposure, focal length, and MakerNote (including WB gains and color matrix) all silently fall back to defaults with no indication to the user or developer. The finding is real as described; severity is correctly characterised as low for a local tool.

title: as_rational_triplet reads 24 bytes starting at value_off without checking that value_off + 24 is within the file, relying solely on Reader::u32 returning Err on OOB.
description: The six `r.u32(p + N)` calls are individually bounds-checked via `Reader::u32`, so no OOB memory access occurs. However, `p = self.value_off as usize` — on 32-bit WASM, `value_off` (a u32) cast to usize is fine, but `p + 20` could theoretically wrap if `p` is near `usize::MAX`. In practice this is unreachable (file data.len() limits p), but the potential partial result (e.g., n0/d0 read OK but n2/d2 return Err → whole function returns None) is handled correctly. This is a low-severity latent issue worth noting for completeness.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:282-293. Every access goes through `r.u32(p + N)` which calls `self.data.get(off..off + 4)` with a bounds check, returning `Err` on OOB. The `?` operator propagates any `Err` as `None` from the function. The concern about `p + 20` wrapping on 32-bit WASM is theoretical: `value_off` is a u32 from the file, so the maximum value is 0xFFFF_FFFF; cast to u32-usize (same on wasm32), `p + 20` would require p near 0xFFFF_FFFF which would mean the file is 4 GB — impossible in a browser WASM context. No concrete failure scenario exists; all error paths are correctly handled.

title: All errors are unstructured strings; no error taxonomy or typed error enum is used anywhere in the codebase.
description: The `tiff::Result<T>` alias uses `String` as the error type, and errors are produced via a `bail!` macro that calls `format!`. This means callers cannot programmatically distinguish a 'file too small' error from a 'missing required tags' error or a 'compression not supported' error — they can only display the string. Introducing a typed `enum ParseError { FileTooSmall, MissingTag(&'static str), UnsupportedCompression(u16), OutOfBounds(usize) }` would enable structured error handling and cleaner JS error codes.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:7-9. The error type is `String` throughout; `bail!` just formats a string. All errors ultimately reach JS as a `JsError::new(&e)` string at lib.rs:206. The JS side cannot branch on error types. The capability (typed errors) is genuinely missing, and for a WASM API consuming untrusted files where different error types warrant different UX responses, this is a real gap. The fix difficulty is correctly marked complex.

title: BitReader::read_bits and read_huff subtract from self.nbits without checking for underflow, which would panic in debug or wrap in release.
description: `read_bits` calls `self.fill(n)` which ensures `self.nbits >= n`, then subtracts `n`. `read_huff` calls `self.fill(12)` then subtracts `len` (the code length from the Huffman table). If the Huffman table entry has a `len` value of 0 (possible if `build_huff` is ever modified and the `huff[0]` sentinel is wrong), `self.nbits` could underflow. Currently `build_huff` produces lengths 1–12 so this is not a practical bug, but the invariant is implicit. Adding a debug_assert that `len >= 1` inside `read_huff` would make the invariant explicit.
evidence: Verified at decompress.rs:91-103 (`build_huff`) and 150-157 (`read_huff`). `huff[0] = 0xc0c` means length=12 (high byte=0x0c), value=12. All other entries are filled with `((i+1) << 8) | i` for i in 0..11, giving lengths 1 through 12. The Huffman table is a fixed compile-time constant — it cannot be modified at runtime. Since `len` is always 1–12 and `fill(12)` ensures `nbits >= 12 >= len`, underflow is structurally impossible given the current code. The finding relies on a hypothetical future modification to `build_huff`, making it a speculative concern rather than an actual bug.

title: No structured logging or diagnostics channel exists; debug information is currently emitted via JS console.log on the JS side only.
description: The Rust WASM code has no logging infrastructure (no `web-sys console::log` calls, no `log` crate, no `tracing`). Diagnostic information (e.g., which WB path was taken, which IFD tags were found, parse warnings) is completely invisible on the Rust side. Adding `web_sys::console::log_1` calls or the `log` crate with a WASM adapter (`console_log`) would make it possible to diagnose field issues without recompiling with custom debug builds.
evidence: Confirmed by reading all four Rust source files: there are no `web_sys::console` calls, no `log` crate imports, and no tracing instrumentation anywhere in the Rust code. The JS side (worker.js) does have console logging, but internal Rust decisions (which WB code path ran, whether color matrix was parsed, parse warnings for corrupt sub-IFDs) are invisible without a debug recompile. The capability is genuinely absent. Given that the codebase already exposes `wb_r_used`, `color_matrix_from_mn`, and timing fields in `ProcessResult`, the pattern of surfacing diagnostics is established — logging is the natural next step.

title: strip_end computed as usize addition can silently overflow on wasm32
description: On wasm32, `usize` is 32-bit. `info.strip_offset as usize + info.strip_byte_count as usize` can overflow if both values are large (e.g. strip_offset near 2 GB), producing a small wrapped value that passes the `strip_end > data.len()` bounds check even though the true end is out of range. A malformed or adversarial ORF can exploit this to pass the safety check and allow subsequent slice indexing to produce an incorrect slice, potentially causing incorrect output or—depending on the allocator—a panic. The fix is to use `u32::checked_add` on the raw u32 values before casting, or `usize::checked_add` and map the None case to an error.
evidence: Verified at lib.rs:217-221. Both `info.strip_offset` and `info.strip_byte_count` are u32 fields, cast to usize (32-bit on wasm32) before addition. If their sum exceeds 0xFFFF_FFFF, the addition wraps silently in release mode, producing a `strip_end` smaller than `strip_offset`. The subsequent bound check `strip_end > data.len()` passes (wrapped small value < data.len()), and `&data[info.strip_offset as usize..strip_end]` would have `start > end`, which panics in Rust with 'slice index starts at X but ends at Y' — so this is a panic rather than silent data corruption, but it bypasses the intended error message and is exploitable with a crafted file. The severity is correctly high for a public WASM API processing untrusted input.

title: Running-average update `diff * 3` can overflow i32
description: `diff` is computed as `(acarry[parity][0] ^ sign) + acarry[parity][1]` where both operands are i32. `acarry[parity][0]` is `(high << nbits) | literal_bits`; with high up to 4094 and nbits up to 15, it can be ~134 million, making `diff` similarly large. Multiplying by 3 in `(diff * 3 + acarry[parity][1]) >> 5` then silently overflows i32 (max ~2.1 billion; 3 × 134M = 402M, which fits, but if two accumulations compound, wrap is possible for pathological streams). The overflow does not panic in release mode (Rust wraps in release), corrupting the running average and producing wrong pixel predictions for all subsequent pixels in the column parity lane until a row reset. dcraw uses `int` with the same expression, so the bug mirrors dcraw, but is still a defect.
code_excerpt: acarry[parity][1] = (diff * 3 + acarry[parity][1]) >> 5;
evidence: Analyzed the range of `diff` more carefully. `acarry[parity][0]` is `(high << nbits) | literal_bits`. `high` comes from `read_huff` and is at most 11 (escape code 12 produces a separate computed value). For non-escape: high ≤ 11, nbits ≤ 2+15=17, but `while nbits < 16` caps nbits at 15; so max is `11 << 15 = 360448`. The sign XOR and the add of `acarry[parity][1]` (which is bounded by `>> 5` each iteration) keeps diff well within i32 range. The escape path (`high0 == 12`) reads `(16-nbits)` bits and right-shifts by 1, bounding the escape high to at most `(1 << 15) >> 1 = 16384`. So `diff` is bounded to roughly ±400K, making `diff * 3` at most ~1.2M — well within i32 range. The compounding concern is also bounded since `acarry[parity][1]` itself is updated as `(diff*3 + acarry[parity][1]) >> 5`, which is a damped IIR filter that converges. No concrete overflow scenario exists.

title: Inline SHORT[2] handling in parse_image_processing_subifd uses unconditional to_le_bytes(), breaking BE files
description: When `cnt == 2` the code reconstructs the two inline SHORTs from `val` by calling `val.to_le_bytes()` unconditionally, then conditionally re-interprets with `u16::from_le_bytes` or `u16::from_be_bytes`. However `val` was already decoded from the file by `r.u32(e + 8)` which applied the file endianness, producing a host-native integer. Calling `to_le_bytes()` on a native integer and then reading the bytes as BE when `r.le == false` reverses the bytes a second time, swapping R and B WB gains and producing inverted color balance for any BE-endian ORF (magic bytes `MMOR`/`MMMR`). On LE files (all Olympus ORFs in practice) the result is correct. The correct approach is to directly extract halves from `val` using bit shifts, as already done consistently everywhere else in the file (e.g. the `inline_u16` closure in `parse_olympus_makernote`).
evidence: This is the same defect as errors-s1t2u3, analysed from the logic perspective with a higher severity. The code at tiff.rs:513 calls `val.to_le_bytes()` unconditionally on a host-native u32 (already endian-decoded). For BE files, `val` holds bytes in host native order but `to_le_bytes()` misinterprets them, then the conditional BE re-interpretation double-swaps, producing wrong R/B values. The comparison to the correct pattern at tiff.rs:398-403 (tag 0x1029, using bit-shift extraction `val & 0xFFFF` / `val >> 16`) confirms the inconsistency. This is a duplicate of errors-s1t2u3 — both describe the same lines and same bug.

title: IfdEntry::as_u32 treats value_off as a file pointer for dtype==3, count==2 (inline case)
description: TIFF spec §7 states that when `count * sizeof(type) <= 4`, the value is stored inline in the value_offset field, not as a pointer. For `dtype == 3` (SHORT, 2 bytes each), `count == 2` means 4 bytes total — all inline. `as_u32` handles `count == 1` inline correctly, but for `count > 1` it falls through to `r.u16(self.value_off as usize)`, treating `value_off` as a file pointer. Any caller that uses `as_u32` on a SHORT[2] entry will dereference an arbitrary file offset (the packed value of the two SHORTs) instead of reading the actual data. In practice IFD0 tags resolved via `as_u32` (width, height, orientation, etc.) all have count==1 so this path is currently not triggered by the parser; however it is a latent correctness defect if new tags are added or files with non-standard count==2 for those tags are encountered.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:235-254. The `count > 1` branch for dtype==3 at line 249 calls `r.u16(self.value_off as usize)`, treating `value_off` as a file pointer. For count==2, TIFF spec requires the two SHORTs to be stored inline in the 4-byte value field (not as an offset). The inline extraction pattern exists correctly for count==1 (lines 242-246) and is also correctly implemented in `parse_olympus_makernote`'s `inline_u16` closure. However `as_u32` for count==2 with dtype==3 reads from the file at offset `value_off` instead of decoding the inline value. Currently no callers of `as_u32` encounter this for SHORT[2] tags (IFD0 tags are all count==1 for the matched tags), confirming this is latent. Real but currently unexploited.

title: Sub-IFD offset calculation uses unchecked u32 addition that can overflow
description: In `parse_olympus_makernote`, for sub-IFDs 0x2010, 0x2020, and 0x2040, the absolute offset is computed as `base_off as u32 + val`. Both `base_off` and `val` are derived from file offsets (each fitting comfortably in u32 for files under 4 GB), but their sum could exceed u32::MAX for a crafted file, wrapping silently and causing the sub-IFD parse to read from a wrong location (likely producing garbage metadata rather than a panic, since bounds checks exist in the readers). Confidence is medium because real ORF files are small enough that overflow is unreachable in practice.
evidence: Verified at tiff.rs:363-367 (actual lines in code are 363, 367, 418 for 0x2040). `base_off as u32 + val` is a u32 addition that wraps silently in release mode. `base_off` is the MakerNote start offset (could be hundreds of KB into the file), and `val` is a 32-bit value from the file. A crafted ORF with `val = 0xFFFFFFFF - base_off + 1` wraps the sub-IFD offset to 0, causing subsequent sub-IFD parsing to read from the file header. Sub-IFD parse functions check bounds via `r.u16()` so no memory safety issue arises, but WB/lens metadata could be silently read from wrong locations. The impact is wrong metadata for adversarial inputs.

title: Huffman escape path: extra bits = 16 - nbits, but when nbits >= 16 saturating_sub gives 0, silently outputting high=0
description: When `high0 == 12` (escape code), the code reads `extra = (16u32).saturating_sub(nbits as u32)` bits. `nbits` can reach 16 (starting at 2+i and growing in the while loop up to the guard `nbits < 16` — but the loop body does `nbits += 1` so nbits exits the loop at exactly 16 when carry is large). At nbits==16, `saturating_sub` returns 0, `read_bits(0)` returns 0, and the escape value becomes 0 after the right-shift. This silently drops the escape payload instead of reading the expected number of bits, corrupting pixel values for any row with a high carry value that triggers the escape code. dcraw reads `(16 - nbits)` unconditionally; when nbits==16 that expression is 0 in C too, so the behaviors match — but it means escape at nbits==16 always yields 0, which may be an inherited dcraw quirk worth verifying against real ORF data.
evidence: Analyzed decompress.rs carefully. `nbits` starts at `2 + i` where `i` is 0 or 2 (lines 26-27), so starts at 2 or 4. The while loop at line 30 increments nbits while `carry_lo >> (nbits + i) > 0` AND `nbits < 16`. The guard `nbits < 16` means nbits never exceeds 15 at loop exit (the condition is checked before the increment). Then `read_bits(3)` at line 34 does not touch `nbits` (it's a separate counter in `br`, not the local `nbits`). So local `nbits` is always in range 2–15 when the escape path is reached. `16 - nbits` is always 1–14; `saturating_sub` never returns 0 here. The finder's claim that 'nbits exits the loop at exactly 16' is wrong because the while condition `nbits < 16` prevents that. This matches dcraw behavior and is correct.

title: apply_unsharp_mask clamps to 4095 but rgb16 values can be full u16 range after apply_look path
description: In `apply_unsharp_mask`, the result is clamped to `0..4095`, which is correct when processing freshly demosaiced 12-bit sensor data (max raw value 4095). However, `apply_unsharp_masks` is also called from `apply_look` in lib.rs with a `rgb16` buffer that was unpacked from cached lightbox bytes — those bytes were produced from the same demosaiced data, so the values are still at most 4095. The clamp is therefore safe today, but the function signature accepts any `&mut [u16]` and the clamp upper bound is a hardcoded magic number with no documentation. If the pipeline is ever extended to process HDR or 14-bit data, the clamp silently destroys values 4096–65535. A named constant or a parameter would make the intent explicit and avoid a future regression.
evidence: Verified at pipeline.rs:209-218 and lib.rs:430-433. The `apply_look` path unpacks rgb16 from `rgb16_bytes` stored as `u16 LE` bytes (6 bytes/pixel), which were produced by `downscale_rgb16_impl` at lib.rs:172-176 — that function averages u16 values from `demosaic_rggb` output which are clamped to `0..0xFFFF` (line 94 in demosaic.rs), but the actual values fed to it come from 12-bit raw data so stay ≤4095. The finder acknowledges 'values are still at most 4095' and correctly frames this as a future-proofing concern rather than a current bug. This is a speculative/opportunity finding, not a concrete failure — consistent with false_positive classification.

title: No property-based or round-trip tests on the Huffman decompressor math-heavy code
description: The decompressor contains several interacting stateful components (carry state, Huffman table, predictor, bit reader) with tricky edge cases at row boundaries, escape codes, and carry overflow. There are no unit tests or property-based tests (e.g. via proptest) verifying that known-good pixel patterns survive a round-trip or that specific dcraw reference inputs produce expected outputs. Given that pixel value bugs are silent (output looks plausible but is subtly wrong), fuzz or property tests comparing against a reference implementation would provide high confidence. Similarly, `pipeline.rs` math (tone curve, color matrix, saturation) is untested, making it hard to detect regressions from slider logic changes.
evidence: Confirmed by searching all Rust source files: there are no `#[test]` functions, no `#[cfg(test)]` modules, no test files in src/, and no test dependencies (proptest, quickcheck) visible. The decompressor, demosaicker, and pipeline are all entirely untested. Given that the decompressor is a port of dcraw with several subtle invariants (carry state, escape code handling, predictors), the absence of even a single reference-output test is a genuine gap. For a tool processing untrusted binary blobs, this is a meaningful quality risk.

title: Inline-vs-pointer discrimination for TIFF value fields is duplicated across multiple parse functions
description: The logic for deciding whether a TIFF entry's value is inline (count * sizeof(type) <= 4) or a file pointer is scattered across `IfdEntry::as_u32`, `parse_olympus_makernote` (the `inline_u16` closure), `parse_image_processing_subifd`, and the camera-settings parser. Each site makes slightly different decisions about which counts are inline (e.g. `cnt <= 2` vs `cnt == 2` vs `cnt == 1`), creating inconsistencies and potential future bugs. A single `fn tiff_value_bytes(entry, reader) -> Result<SmallVec<[u8;4]>>` or similar helper that centralises the inline/pointer dispatch would eliminate the duplication and the discrepancy in the SHORT[2] case found in issue `logic-a3b4c5d6`.
evidence: Verified in tiff.rs: `as_u32` (line 237) uses count==1 for inline SHORT; the `inline_u16` closure (line 346) uses cnt<=2; `parse_image_processing_subifd` (line 511) uses cnt==2 for inline; `parse_camera_settings_subifd` (line 484) always reads inline. The inconsistency is real and structural — four distinct dispatch patterns with no shared utility.

title: separable_blur allocates two full-image buffers and has an inner kernel loop that defeats LLVM auto-vectorization
description: For a 20MP image with the 13-tap clarity kernel, separable_blur executes ~780M multiply-adds across two passes (H-pass then V-pass), each touching width×height×3×13 iterations. The innermost loop iterates over the kernel with a per-iteration clamped index calculation `(x as isize + ki as isize - half as isize).clamp(...)` that introduces a data-dependent address computation per tap, preventing LLVM from emitting SIMD gather/load sequences. Two `vec![0u16; n]` allocations of ~120MB each (for a 20MP image: 5472×3648×3×2 bytes = ~120MB) occur on every blur call. When both texture and clarity are active, four such allocations happen back-to-back.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 173-207 match exactly: `temp = vec![0u16; n]` and `out = vec![0u16; n]` are both allocated on every call, and the inner kernel loop uses a clamp-based isize index computation per tap (line 183-185) that prevents SIMD vectorization. Two large heap allocations per `separable_blur` call is confirmed.

title: process() rebuilds three 65536-entry pre-LUTs and one 65536-entry post-LUT on every apply_look call, totalling ~384KB of computation at interactive slider rates
description: Each call to `process()` invokes `build_pre_lut` three times and `build_post_lut` once. Each LUT iterates 65536 entries: the pre-LUT does two float multiplies and a clamp per entry; the post-LUT calls `tone_curve` which contains up to three `powf` calls (shadows gamma, highlights gamma, sRGB EOTF). At 60fps drag on a lightbox image, `apply_look` calls `process` on every animation frame, re-evaluating all four LUTs each time regardless of which slider moved. The powf calls inside `build_post_lut` are especially costly: at 65536 iterations with potential 3 powf each, that is up to ~196K `powf` calls per frame.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 241-252: `build_pre_lut` is called three times and `build_post_lut` once unconditionally in every `process()` call. `build_post_lut` (line 154-161) iterates 65536 entries calling `tone_curve`, which calls `powf` up to 3 times per entry (lines 115, 123, 81). No caching or param-change detection exists; every `apply_look` call triggers a full rebuild.

title: apply_unsharp_masks calls separable_blur twice, allocating and immediately discarding up to four full-image buffers (~480MB total for 20MP)
description: When both texture and clarity are non-zero, `apply_unsharp_masks` calls `apply_unsharp_mask` twice, each of which calls `separable_blur`, which itself allocates two `Vec<u16>` of `width * height * 3` elements. For a 20MP image that is 4 × 120MB = ~480MB of allocation and deallocation per re-render triggered by slider drag. These buffers cannot be reused across the two calls because each has different dimensions (same here, since full-image) but the blur intermediates are discarded after the sharpening blend. The allocations also stress WASM's linear memory, which cannot release pages back to the OS.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 220-228 and 209-218 match exactly. When both texture and clarity are nonzero, `apply_unsharp_mask` is called twice, each calling `separable_blur` which allocates two full-image `Vec<u16>` buffers. The allocation pattern is confirmed; the WASM linear memory concern is accurate and real.

title: Channel-interleaved loop order in separable_blur prevents auto-vectorization across channels
description: The innermost loop structure is `y → x → c → ki`, where `c` iterates over 3 channels inside the pixel loop. This interleaving means each channel's accumulation is mixed with index computations for the other channels, preventing LLVM from widening across the 3-channel dimension. Reordering to `c → y → x → ki` (with planar intermediate buffers) would allow the `x` inner loop to be a contiguous SIMD-friendly reduction. With WASM SIMD (128-bit, 4 f32 lanes), the kernel accumulation loop over 5 or 13 taps could be vectorized across 4 output pixels at once, giving a theoretical 4x speedup on the inner loop.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 178-190 match exactly: loop order is y→x→c→ki with interleaved channel writes into an RGB-interleaved buffer. The stride-3 access pattern (`src[(y*width+xi)*3+c]`) in the innermost loop makes auto-vectorization across channels impossible. This is a real structural vectorization blocker.

title: downscale_rgb16_impl recomputes floating-point row/column bounds (x0, x1, y0, y1) per output pixel using f32 arithmetic that is not hoisted out of the column loop
description: In `downscale_rgb16_impl`, the vertical span `y0` and `y1` depends only on `dy` and `yr`, so it could be computed once per row. However, `x0` and `x1` recompute `dx as f32 * xr` and `((dx as f32) + 1.0) * xr` per pixel. For a 5472×3648 → 1800×1200 downscale, the outer loop runs 1200 times and the inner loop 1800 times, performing 3600 f32 multiplies for bounds that could be integer-incremented. The `y0..y1` and `x0..x1` inner loops also recompute the flat index `y * sw + x` without hoisting the `y * sw` stride, causing an extra multiply per source pixel in the box.
evidence: lib.rs lines 148-165 confirm: y0/y1 are correctly hoisted to the outer dy loop (lines 149-151), but x0/x1 are recomputed per dx (lines 153-155), and the inner loop at line 160 computes `(y * sw + x) * 3` without hoisting `y * sw`. The description is accurate. Severity is genuinely low (LLVM strength-reduction likely handles the multiply), but the redundancy is real.

title: apply_look unpacks rgb16 bytes into a Vec<u16> using a per-element map/collect, missing a safe bytemuck or copy_from_slice path
description: The `apply_look` function unpacks `rgb16_bytes` (6 bytes per pixel, packed u16 LE) into `Vec<u16>` using `(0..n).map(|i| u16::from_le_bytes([rgb16_bytes[i*2], rgb16_bytes[i*2+1]])).collect()`. This generates an iterator that accesses two non-adjacent bytes per step and builds a Vec element-by-element, with bounds checks on each pair. A `bytemuck::cast_slice` (zero-copy reinterpret on LE platforms, which WASM is) or a `u16::from_le_bytes` loop with `chunks_exact(2)` would be more efficient and still safe. For a 1800×1200 lightbox buffer that is 2.16M elements being individually converted on every slider drag.
evidence: lib.rs lines 430-433 match exactly. The index-based map/collect pattern with `i*2` and `i*2+1` is confirmed; LLVM can auto-vectorize `chunks_exact(2)` more reliably than the index arithmetic form. The `bytemuck` approach would require a dependency but `chunks_exact(2)` is stdlib. Real and actionable, though the actual performance gap is likely small since LLVM will often optimize this.

title: rgb_to_rgba pushes one byte at a time rather than using extend_from_slice or unsafe ptr copy
description: The `rgb_to_rgba` function pushes 4 bytes per pixel individually into a pre-capacity Vec. While LLVM will likely vectorize 4 sequential `push` calls on a Vec with spare capacity, the explicit `push` API still goes through the Vec growth boundary check on each call. Using `out.extend_from_slice(&[r, g, b, 255])` or building the array inline and writing 4 bytes at once via a `chunks_mut(4)` approach is cleaner and more reliably vectorized. For a 20MP image this is 20M pixels × 4 pushes = 80M individual calls through the Vec::push code path.
evidence: lib.rs lines 474-484 match exactly. The Vec is pre-allocated with `with_capacity(n*4)` so no reallocation occurs, and LLVM is likely to optimize consecutive bounded pushes into a single 4-byte store, but `extend_from_slice(&[r,g,b,255])` is a cleaner and more explicitly vectorizable pattern. Real finding at low severity; the claim about '80M individual Vec::push code path calls' is somewhat overstated since LLVM inlines and vectorizes, but the improvement opportunity is real.

title: process() inner pixel loop computes luma and vibrance weighting with floating-point per pixel even when vibrance is zero
description: For every pixel, `process` computes `luma`, `raw_mx`, `mx`, `mn`, `pixel_sat`, and `vib_w` unconditionally, regardless of whether `vibrance` (or `params.vibrance`) is zero. When vibrance is 0, `vib_w` is unused in the final scale (`vib * vib_w * 0.6` = 0), making min/max/saturation computation dead work. For a 20MP image that is 20M iterations of `r2.max(g2).max(b2)` + `r2.min(g2).min(b2)` + one divide and one clamp that could be skipped with a simple branch on `vib != 0.0`. Similarly, saturation applies a per-pixel luma computation every time even when `sat == 1.0` (no-op scale).
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 275-284 confirm: luma, raw_mx, mx, mn, pixel_sat, vib_w are all computed unconditionally for every pixel regardless of `vib` value. However, note that `sat` has a non-zero baseline (BASELINE_SAT=1.20), so the luma computation is always needed for saturation; only the vibrance-specific min/max/pixel_sat could be guarded. The finding is real but the description slightly overstates — luma cannot be skipped even when vib==0.

title: build_post_lut calls tone_curve 65536 times, each potentially calling powf up to 3 times, totalling up to ~196K powf calls per process() invocation
description: Inside `build_post_lut`, `tone_curve` is called for each of 65536 LUT entries. `tone_curve` conditionally calls `powf` for shadows gamma, highlights gamma (both guarded by `abs() > 1e-4`), and the sRGB EOTF (`linear_to_srgb` which calls `powf(1.0/2.4)` for non-tiny values). In the common case (shadows != 0, highlights != 0, most values > 0.0031308), all three fire: ~196K `powf` calls per build. `powf` in wasm32 is a software fallback (no native instruction), making it significantly more expensive than on native x86 with `vrsqrtss` or hardware approximations. A fixed-point polynomial approximation or a two-segment approximation for the sRGB EOTF would eliminate most of this cost.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 102-161 confirm: `tone_curve` calls `y.powf(gamma)` (line 115), `inv.powf(gamma)` (line 123), and `linear_to_srgb` (line 139) which calls `v.powf(1.0/2.4)` (line 81) for values above 0.0031308. All three are called in the common case. `build_post_lut` calls `tone_curve` for all 65536 entries unconditionally.

title: LUTs in process() are pure functions of params and could be cached across apply_look calls when params are unchanged
description: The four LUTs (`pre_r`, `pre_g`, `pre_b`, `post`) are entirely determined by `PipelineParams`. On every slider drag event, `apply_look` is called with params that typically differ in only one field (e.g. contrast changes the post-LUT but not the pre-LUTs; WB changes only the pre-LUTs). Caching the LUTs on the JS side as `Uint16Array` / `Uint8Array` blobs and passing them in, or moving LUT construction into a separate WASM export (`build_luts(params) -> LutHandle`), would allow `apply_look` to skip LUT rebuild when the relevant params are unchanged. This is the highest-leverage single change for interactive responsiveness.
evidence: The LUTs are pure functions of PipelineParams fields (confirmed in pipeline.rs lines 241-252). No caching mechanism exists in the current code. The observation that only one LUT group changes per slider interaction is architecturally correct: pre-LUTs depend on wb/exposure/black/white/temp/tint; post-LUT depends on contrast/shadows/highlights/whites/blacks. The opportunity is real and high-value.

title: process() inner pixel loop is a good candidate for WASM SIMD via std::arch::wasm32
description: The per-pixel loop in `process` does a 3×3 matrix multiply, 3-component luma dot product, and per-channel scale — all branchy-free floating-point that maps well to `f32x4` WASM SIMD intrinsics. The LUT lookups (scatter/gather) cannot be vectorized, but the matrix and saturation stages before the lookup could process 4 pixels at once using `v128` instructions. Given the loop runs 20M times for a full-resolution render, even a partial vectorization of the matrix stage would be impactful. This requires `wasm32` target feature `simd128` and explicit intrinsics (auto-vectorization is unlikely due to the LUT gather at the end).
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 264-293 confirm the loop structure: 3×3 matrix multiply + luma dot product + min/max saturation per pixel, followed by LUT gather lookups. The matrix and saturation stages (lines 270-284) are indeed branchless float arithmetic suitable for f32x4 WASM SIMD. The LUT gather (lines 287-292) blocks full vectorization. This is a real opportunity with meaningful but bounded complexity.

title: rotate_180 copies pixels three bytes at a time instead of as a u32 or u24 word
description: The `rotate_180` function iterates columns and copies 3 bytes individually (`dst_row[c*3]`, `dst_row[c*3+1]`, `dst_row[c*3+2]`). A single `dst_row[c*3..c*3+3].copy_from_slice(&s_row[sc*3..sc*3+3])` would let LLVM emit a 3-byte or 4-byte load-store, and the reversed-column copy could also be split into a row-reversal using chunks of 3 bytes via a `rev()` chunk iterator. For a 20MP image rotated 180°, this is 60M byte copies. The current form is unlikely to auto-vectorize because of the non-contiguous scatter pattern (reversed column index).
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 400-415 match exactly. The three individual byte assignments per pixel (lines 410-412) are confirmed. The reversed column index `sc = w - 1 - c` does prevent straightforward auto-vectorization. `copy_from_slice` for 3-byte chunks would be cleaner, though actual speedup depends on whether LLVM can already merge the three adjacent stores.

title: Decompressor recomputes multiple raw[row*width + col ± 2] index expressions redundantly for the north-west predictor
description: In the main decompression loop, when `row >= 2 && col >= 2`, three reads occur: `out[row*width + col - 2]`, `out[(row-2)*width + col]`, and `out[(row-2)*width + col - 2]`. The multiplications `row*width` and `(row-2)*width` are recomputed from scratch each iteration rather than being maintained as incremented row-offset variables. LLVM's strength-reduction should handle this, but the branch structure (three separate `if row < 2 / col < 2` guards) adds branch cost that could be resolved by precomputing `row_base = row * width` and `prev_row_base = (row - 2) * width` outside the column loop. Low severity since decompression is a one-shot operation.
evidence: decompress.rs lines 61-82 match: `row * width` is recomputed in the col-loop body at lines 64, 66, 68, 69, 70, and `(row-2) * width` at lines 66, 69, 70. LLVM strength-reduction likely eliminates the multiply, but hoisting `row_base` and `prev_row_base` is still a valid micro-optimization. The one-shot nature of decompression correctly keeps this low severity.

title: Timing instrumentation covers only decompress/demosaic/tonemap/orient but not the two downscale_rgb16_impl calls or apply_unsharp_masks
description: The `now_ms()` timing in `process_orf` wraps decompress, demosaic, and tonemap+orient, but `downscale_rgb16_impl` (called twice, for lb and thumb) and `apply_unsharp_masks` (called before `process`) are not individually timed. Given that a 20MP → 1800px downscale with a box-filter over ~9 source pixels per output pixel is ~29M pixel reads, and unsharp masks dominate at ~480MB of allocation, profiling data for these stages would be valuable for optimization prioritization.
evidence: lib.rs confirms: `decompress_ms`, `demosaic_ms`, `tonemap_ms`, and `orient_ms` are measured (lines 223-313), but the two `downscale_rgb16_impl` calls (lines 272, 282) and the `apply_unsharp_masks` call (line 304) have no timing wrappers. The gap is exactly as described.

title: strip_offset + strip_byte_count can overflow usize on wasm32, defeating the bounds guard
description: On wasm32, usize is 32 bits. Both strip_offset and strip_byte_count are u32 values read from the attacker-controlled ORF file. Their sum on line 217 is computed as usize arithmetic, so if strip_offset = 0xFFFFFFF0 and strip_byte_count = 0x20, strip_end wraps to 0x10. The guard on line 218 (`strip_end > data.len()`) then passes for any file >= 16 bytes. The subsequent slice `&data[info.strip_offset as usize..strip_end]` has start > end and Rust panics (WASM trap), producing a reliable denial-of-service. No memory unsafety occurs because the safe-Rust slice panics, but an attacker can trap the WASM worker with a crafted header.
evidence: lib.rs lines 217-221 match exactly. Both `strip_offset` and `strip_byte_count` are `u32` fields (tiff.rs lines 19-20) cast to usize before addition, with no overflow check. On wasm32 (32-bit usize), the addition can wrap. The wrap causes `strip_end < strip_offset`, making the slice `start > end` which panics in safe Rust, causing a DoS trap. Fix: use `(info.strip_offset as usize).checked_add(info.strip_byte_count as usize)`.

title: get_unchecked in demosaic `at()` is safe for normal images but the assert does not prevent stride-overflow OOB on wasm32
description: The assertion `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)` (line 27) and the zero-dimension guard in lib.rs together mean `at()` is safe for well-formed inputs. However, on wasm32 where usize=u32, if an attacker supplies width and height both close to 65535, then `width * height` wraps during the assert (producing the same wrapped value that `decompress` used to allocate `out`), and the assert passes. Inside the demosaic loop, `row * width + col` also wraps identically, so `get_unchecked` aliases within the allocated buffer rather than escaping it — no true OOB in practice. The real residual risk is that the `unsafe` block provides no auditable proof of safety without studying all callers, and any future change that modifies allocation size without updating the assert would silently introduce UB. Severity is medium because current call chains are safe, but the unsafe is structurally fragile.
evidence: demosaic.rs lines 11-14 confirmed. The `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)` at line 27 does overflow-match `decompress`'s `vec![0u16; width * height]`, so on wasm32 a wrapped allocation means the assert passes and `get_unchecked` aliasing stays within bounds. However, the caller (lib.rs line 259) only reaches demosaic after checking `width != 0 && height != 0` (line 108), but there is no cap on width/height values from the file, so the overflow scenario is real for crafted files. The finding is real but correctly self-assessed as 'no true OOB in current code' — the risk is future-regression fragility of the unsafe block.

title: as_ascii value_off + count addition can overflow u32, silently truncating parsed strings
description: In IfdEntry::as_ascii, the range `self.value_off as usize .. (self.value_off + self.count) as usize` computes the addition in u32 before casting. If value_off is 0xFFFFFF00 and count is 0x200, the sum wraps to 0xFF. The .get() call then returns None (start > end is caught), so the string is silently empty rather than causing a panic. While not a memory safety issue, this means crafted tag values can cause critical metadata (make/model/lens) to be silently lost, potentially bypassing downstream logic that relies on those fields. Should be computed as `(self.value_off as usize).saturating_add(self.count as usize)`.
code_excerpt:
let bytes = r
            .data
            .get(self.value_off as usize..(self.value_off + self.count) as usize)
            .unwrap_or(&[]);
        String::from_utf8_lossy(bytes)
evidence: tiff.rs lines 261-265 confirm: `(self.value_off + self.count)` is computed as u32 addition (both fields are u32) before casting to usize. If both are large and sum wraps, the resulting range has end < start and `.get()` returns `None`, silently yielding empty string. The fix is `(self.value_off as usize).saturating_add(self.count as usize)`. Real but not a memory-safety issue.

title: base_off as u32 + val sub-IFD offset arithmetic overflows u32 in MakerNote parsing
description: In parse_olympus_makernote, sub-IFD offsets for Equipment (0x2010), CameraSettings (0x2020), and ImageProcessing (0x2040) are computed as `base_off as u32 + val` before being passed to sub-IFD parsers. base_off is a usize cast to u32 (truncating on 64-bit), and val is attacker-controlled from the ORF. If the sum overflows u32, the resulting offset is passed to the sub-IFD parser which does a `p + 2 > r.data.len()` check, but with a wrapped (small) p value the check passes and the parser reads IFD entries from near offset 0 of the file. This causes wrong data to be parsed as WB/lens metadata — a data-confusion rather than memory-safety issue. Each sub-parser is otherwise bounds-checked.
evidence: tiff.rs lines 363, 367, 418 confirm: `base_off as u32 + val` is computed in u32 arithmetic (where `base_off: usize` is cast to u32 and `val: u32` is attacker-controlled). On wasm32 this is always 32-bit; on 64-bit hosts the cast truncates. The overflow wraps the offset to a small value, which passes the `p + 2 > r.data.len()` guard in sub-parsers (lines 446, 474, 493), causing reads from near file offset 0 — data confusion but no memory safety violation. Real finding.

title: downscale_rgb length check overflows usize on wasm32 for large attacker-supplied dimensions
description: The exported WASM function downscale_rgb accepts src_w and src_h from JavaScript without upper-bound validation. On wasm32, `sw * sh * 3` can overflow usize when dimensions are large (e.g. src_w=50000, src_h=50000: 50000*50000*3 = 7.5B > 4GB). The overflow makes the length check on line 367 compare against a wrapped value, allowing it to pass with a mismatched src buffer. The inner loop then accesses `src[(y * sw + x) * 3 + 2]`, which Rust's bounds checker traps — producing a WASM panic rather than memory corruption, but enabling reliable DoS from JS. A similar overflow exists in the output allocation `dw * dh * 3`.
evidence: lib.rs lines 366-372 confirm: `sw * sh * 3` is usize multiplication with no overflow check on line 367, and `dw * dh * 3` similarly on line 372. On wasm32, both can wrap for large JS-supplied dimensions. The length check uses the wrapped value, potentially passing incorrectly; subsequent inner-loop accesses will panic via Rust's bounds checker causing a WASM trap / DoS. The `downscale_rgb16_impl` internal function (line 144) has the same issue but is only called with internally-computed dimensions.

title: apply_look does not validate that rgb16_bytes length matches the declared width×height
description: The exported apply_look function accepts width and height from JavaScript and derives rgb16 by dividing rgb16_bytes.len() by 2, ignoring whether the resulting length matches width*height*3. When apply_unsharp_masks or process is called with these dimensions, separable_blur allocates `width*height*3` entries but accesses `src[(y*width+xi)*3+c]` using the caller-supplied width/height. If rgb16_bytes is shorter than the dimensions imply, these accesses panic (WASM trap). An adversarial JS caller can trivially trigger this by passing a 6-byte buffer with width=1000, height=1000. Adding a check `if rgb16.len() != w * h * 3 { return Err(...) }` before the processing stage would fix this.
evidence: separable_blur (pipeline.rs:185) indexes src[(y*width+xi)*3+c] using caller-supplied width/height against a src slice whose length is derived solely from rgb16_bytes.len()/2. If JS passes a 6-byte buffer with width=1000&height=1000, rgb16.len()=3 but the loop accesses indices up to ~3M, causing an immediate WASM panic/trap. process() is safe (derives n from rgb16.len()/3) but apply_unsharp_masks is not. No length cross-check exists anywhere between lines 430–466.

title: read_ifd allocates Vec with attacker-controlled count up to 65535 entries without bounding
description: read_ifd reads a u16 entry count directly from the ORF file and calls Vec::with_capacity(count). A crafted ORF can set count=65535, causing a ~786 KB allocation per IFD. This function is called for IFD0, the EXIF IFD, and the GPS IFD — three potential allocations of this size per file.
