While each individual allocation is modest, combined with the fact that subsequent iterations attempt to read 12 bytes per entry (each bounds-checked), the main concern is CPU time for large counts pointing past end-of-file: the loop fails gracefully but iterates up to 65535 times with a bounds check each iteration. This is a low-severity DoS.
evidence: Code at tiff.rs:298-308 exactly matches: count is read directly as u16 (max 65535) and passed to Vec::with_capacity without any upper-bound clamp. The loop terminates early via ? when reads go out of bounds, but a crafted file large enough to contain 65535×12=786 KB of IFD data causes both the full allocation and full iteration. Severity is accurately characterized as low — each individual worst-case is ~786 KB and the loop is bounded; this is a resource-amplification issue, not a crash.

title: BitReader.fill() accumulates nbits past data end, risking u32 overflow on pathologically large images
description: When the compressed stream is exhausted, fill() returns synthetic 0 bytes and increments nbits by 8 on each call. For an attacker-crafted ORF with valid width/height (e.g. 4096x4096 = 16M pixels) but a near-empty compressed stream, the decompressor loops 16M times, each iteration calling fill() multiple times and accumulating nbits. On a 32-bit wasm target, nbits (u32) overflows after ~536M bits of phantom fill. After overflow, `self.buf >> (self.nbits - n)` computes with a wrapped nbits value; Rust's u32 subtraction wraps in release mode, and the shift amount modulo 64 produces arbitrary bit patterns. This corrupts pixel values silently rather than panicking. In practice wasm memory limits cap image sizes, so exploitation requires an extremely large declared dimension.
evidence: The overflow mechanism is real: nbits is u32 and is never reset between rows, so feeding phantom 0-bytes past EOF accumulates it unboundedly. However, the scenario requires approximately 536M/16 ≈ 33M phantom fill calls, which would demand a declared image of ~33M pixels with a near-empty stream — at the practical edge of 32-bit WASM address space (4 GB). The finding correctly describes the arithmetic (u32 wrap → subtraction underflow → shift modulo 64 in release) but the exploitation preconditions are extreme enough that real-world impact is negligible. Downgraded from medium to low in effective severity.

title: No centralized input validation schema for ORF structural constraints (dimensions, offsets, counts)
description: Validation of attacker-controlled fields (width, height, strip_offset, strip_byte_count, IFD entry counts, sub-IFD offsets) is scattered across parse(), read_ifd(), and individual IfdEntry methods, with no single place that enforces documented maximums (e.g. max image dimension 8192, max IFD entries 256). Centralizing these checks into a validation step after tiff::parse() returns would make the security surface auditable and prevent issues like the strip_end overflow and read_ifd large-count DoS. This is a structural hardening opportunity, not a single exploitable bug.
evidence: The existing check at lib.rs:214-221 only guards against zero values; no upper-bound enforcement exists for width, height, or IFD counts anywhere in the codebase. The finding accurately characterizes scattered validation: read_ifd has no count cap, strip_end is checked against data.len() but not against a sane maximum, and sub-IFD offset validation is absent. This is correctly classified as an architectural opportunity rather than a single exploitable bug.

title: No SAST tooling (cargo-audit, cargo-geiger, or Clippy deny) configured in CI for this WASM binary parser
description: The repository processes untrusted binary files (ORF) in a security-sensitive context (WASM exposed to the web) but has no evidence of automated static analysis in CI. cargo-geiger would flag the unsafe block in demosaic.rs, cargo-audit would catch known-vulnerable dependency versions, and `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` at crate root (with a targeted allow for demosaic::at) would make unsafe surface auditable. Adding these as CI steps would catch regressions and new unsafe additions automatically.
evidence: The git status shows no .github/workflows or CI config among tracked or untracked files, confirming no automated SAST pipeline exists. The codebase processes untrusted ORF binary input in a WASM context and contains at least one unsafe block (demosaic.rs) with no #![deny(unsafe_code)] guard at the crate root. The recommendation is accurate and actionable, correctly classified as an info-severity opportunity.
